title: Monthly average surface temperatures by decade
subtitle: The temperature of the air measured 2 meters above the ground, encompassing land, sea, and in-land water surfaces.
caption:
Monthly average surface temperatures by decade

The temperature of the air measured 2 meters above the ground, encompassing land, sea, and in-land water surfaces.

This line chart displays monthly average surface temperatures for the world across nine distinct decades, ranging from the 1940s to the 2020s. The horizontal axis represents the months of the year, numbered 1 through 12 from January to December, while the vertical axis measures temperature in degrees Celsius, spanning from 12°C to 17°C. Each decade is represented by a separate line, color-coded from dark grey for the 1940s to dark red for the 2020s. The lines follow a consistent seasonal curve, rising from the start of the year to a peak around month 7 (July) before declining toward month 12. The decades are vertically stacked, with the 2020s line positioned at the top and the 1940s line at the bottom. The 2020s line reaches the highest point on the chart, peaking just below 17°C in month 7. The 2010s and 2000s lines follow closely beneath, peaking between 16°C and 16.5°C. The lines for the 1990s, 1980s, and 1970s occupy the middle section of the chart, clustering together with peaks near 16°C. The 1960s and 1950s lines sit lower, peaking around 15.5°C to 15.8°C. The 1940s line forms the lowest boundary, peaking just above 15.5°C. In the colder months of January and December, the temperature range across decades spans roughly from 11.8°C to 13.2°C. The note indicates that the decadal average for the 2020s comprises fewer than the typical 10 data points, which may introduce greater uncertainty compared to other decades.
